reason: I searched the entire paper for any argument connecting chunked_invoke to C++ design philosophy (zero-overhead abstraction, don't pay for what you don't use, composability, trust-the-programmer, etc.). The paper motivates the feature purely in terms of convenience and code reduction when calling intrinsics, and the only alignment argument is that the naming matches existing simd functions (chunk, cat). No passage argues how the proposal fits with broader C++ language design principles or philosophy.

Section 4.1.1 discusses a concrete alternative (simd::chunked_invoke_indexed vs. probing) with a concrete rejection reason (precedent set by simd::permute). Section 4.2 also discusses why prototype-based chunking is not supported, with a concrete reason ('complicated and error-prone'). These are internal design alternatives rather than fundamentally different approaches, but the rubric states 'A single concrete alternative with a concrete rejection reason, where the design space is genuinely narrow' suffices for REJECT. Whether the design space here is genuinely narrow enough for these internal variants to satisfy Q7 requires judgment.
